paper.title: Discovery of WINERED-HVS1: A metal-rich hyper-velocity star candidate ejected from the Galactic center

extraction.summary: Paper searches for HVS candidates ejected from the Galactic center using kinematic backward orbit integration with four method variants. It identifies 74 kinematically-selected HVS candidates, all of which the paper explicitly concludes are bound to the Milky Way. From these, 22 metal-rich HVS candidates are selected (9 spectroscopically-confirmed mrHVSspec1-9 and 13 XP-based WINERED-HVS1 + mrHVSxp2-13). The paper repeatedly states that all candidates are bound to the Milky Way, with ejection velocities 500-600 km/s below the Galactic center escape velocity of ~700-800 km/s. Because the paper explicitly judges all candidates as bound to the Galaxy, no candidates meet the Stella inclusion criteria.

source_refs.context: Gaia DR3 catalog used for HVS search

method_chain.summary: Cross-match with chemical abundance catalog from Hattori et al. (2025) on Zenodo

source_refs.context: we cross-match the sample with the chemical abundance catalog

method_chain.summary: Quality criteria cuts on astrometry and XP spectrophotometry
Distance estimates from geometric and photogeometric distances in Gaia DR3

source_refs.context: cross-match with StarHorse catalog to obtain photometric distance information

method_chain.summary: Galactocentric position and velocity calculations with Solar motion corrections
Four methods with different Galactic potential models (axisymmetric and barred) and Solar position assumptions

source_refs.context: Four methods to kinematically search for HVS candidates

method_chain.summary: Backward orbit integration for 200 Myr in Galactic potential to find last disk crossing
Monte Carlo orbit assessment with Conditions 1 and 2 for HVS candidacy, evaluating ejection radius and velocity

source_refs.context: Kinematic conditions for HVS candidate selection

method_chain.summary: Chemical abundance analysis to distinguish Galactic center origin from halo contaminants (GES, Splash)

source_refs.context: Further pruning of HVS candidates with chemistry

candidate_groups_considered.description: 74 kinematically-selected HVS candidates from Gaia DR3 identified via backward orbit integration with four method variants (Methods A-D).
candidate_groups_considered.reason: The paper explicitly concludes that all 74 kinematically-selected HVS candidates are bound to the Milky Way (\citealt{main.tex}:811). Their ejection velocities of 500-600 km/s are below the Galactic center escape velocity of ~700-800 km/s. Stella excludes objects the paper judges bound to the Galaxy.

source_refs.context: Paper states all 74 kinematically-selected HVS candidates are bound to the Milky Way

candidate_groups_considered.description: 9 spectroscopically confirmed metal-rich HVS candidates (mrHVSspec1-9) with literature spectroscopic abundances satisfying [Fe/H]_spec > -1.
candidate_groups_considered.reason: These candidates are a subset of the 74 kinematically-selected candidates, and the paper explicitly states that all 74 candidates are bound to the Milky Way. The paper further suggests these are likely non-HVS contaminants from GES or Splash populations.

source_refs.context: Selection of 9 spectroscopically confirmed metal-rich HVS candidates
Chemical assessment suggesting mrHVSspec1-9 are consistent with non-HVS reference stars

candidate_groups_considered.description: 13 XP-based metal-rich HVS candidates including WINERED-HVS1 and mrHVSxp2-13, selected with Gaia XP chemistry satisfying [M/H]_XP > -1.
candidate_groups_considered.reason: These candidates are also a subset of the 74 kinematically-selected candidates. The paper explicitly states all 74 candidates are bound to the Milky Way. While WINERED-HVS1 is called the 'most promising HVS candidate,' the paper still concludes it is gravitationally bound and 'not an unambiguous HVS candidate from a kinematic perspective.'

source_refs.context: Selection of 13 XP-based metal-rich HVS candidates
Paper states WINERED-HVS1 is gravitationally bound and not an unambiguous HVS candidate from a kinematic perspective
